summary: Drew DeVault argues that the Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO) is a superior alternative to Contributor License Agreements (CLAs) for managing open source contributions. He uses Amazon's OpenSearch fork of ElasticSearch as a positive example, noting that Elastic's use of a CLA enabled their eventual switch to a proprietary license. The DCO, originally designed for the Linux kernel, lets contributors certify they have the right to submit code while retaining their copyright and preventing relicensing. DeVault urges project maintainers to adopt the DCO and advises contributors to refuse signing CLAs.
key_points: CLAs are effectively a promise that project stewards will eventually relicense community contributions under a nonfree license, as Elastic demonstrated
The DCO, built into git via the -s flag, solves legitimate copyright concerns without surrendering contributor rights
Amazon's adoption of the DCO for OpenSearch signals genuine commitment to keeping the project open source by making relicensing extremely difficult
Contributors should withhold work from CLA-requiring projects as a form of principled resistance
money_quotes: In practice, they are a promise that one day the stewards of the codebase will take your work and relicense it under a nonfree license.
A CLA is a promise to the contributors that someday their work will be taken from them and monetized to the exclusive benefit of the project's lords.
You retain your copyright and you don't leave the door open for the maintainers to relicense your work under some other terms in the future.
This offers the maintainers the same rights that they extended to the community themselves.
By doing this, they have imposed on themselves a great deal of difficulty to any future attempt to change their copyright obligations.
Your work is important, and the projects you offer it to should respect that.
key_insight: The DCO protects contributor rights by ensuring contributions stay under the original open source license, while CLAs create an asymmetric power structure that enables proprietary relicensing.
